ORIGINALITY.justification: The paper proposes a distinct representation and prediction strategy—tiling frames into overlapping patches, estimating/predicting per-patch 2x3 affine transforms, and reconstructing frames by applying these transforms—rather than regressing pixels or relying purely on optical flow, which is a novel methodological perspective. It also introduces a practical evaluation protocol (feeding generated frames to a C3D classifier trained on ground truth) that clearly differentiates its contribution from prior work. While the problem (video frame prediction) is well-studied and some components (convolutional predictors, patch-based modeling) are familiar, the combination and focus on affine-transform space prediction is a non-trivial and informative extension that would interest experts.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The paper's main claims are clearly stated and supported by experiments showing the affine-transform model outperforms copy-last-frame, optical-flow extrapolation, and a multi-scale adversarial CNN on UCF-101 while using fewer parameters/FLOPs. The methodology (estimating 2x3 affine transforms per patch, training a convnet in transform space, and reconstructing frames) is technically coherent and mathematical elements are straightforward and complete. Experimental choices and baselines are appropriate, and the authors acknowledge realistic limitations (movement underestimation due to MSE, limited to ~4 frames, ignores out-of-plane/deformable effects), which slightly temper generality but do not invalidate the main results. Given these minor limitations and the scope of evaluation, the technical claims are largely supported.

CLARITY.justification: The paper is well-organized with a clear flow from motivation to method and experiments, and the main contributions are stated early (predicting affine patch transforms and a classifier-based evaluation). Notation and experimental details appear consistent and well-specified (hyperparameters reported, related work coverage adequate), and writing quality is described as good. A minor presentation weakness is that the summary does not indicate whether figures/tables and code are fully detailed for reproducibility, suggesting small gaps in ancillary materials rather than core clarity.

main_claims: Predicting future frames by modeling local affine transformations between patches yields sharper and more plausible video generations than regressing pixels directly.
Propose an evaluation protocol that measures generation quality by feeding generated frames into a classifier trained on ground-truth sequences (C3D) and measuring classification accuracy.

A simple convolutional model operating in affine-transform space outperforms baselines (copy-last-frame, optical-flow extrapolation, and a multi-scale adversarial CNN) on UCF-101 while being computationally cheaper (fewer parameters and FLOPs).
Video frames can be well represented as tiled local affine transforms, enabling accurate reconstruction from estimated transforms.
_summary.methodology: Tile frames into overlapping patches, estimate a 2x3 affine transform per patch by minimizing reconstruction MSE, train a convolutional network to predict future patch-wise affine transforms from past transforms (minimizing MSE in transform space), and reconstruct future frames by applying predicted affine transforms to the last observed frame; multi-step prediction is achieved by replicating/sharing the CNN over time and backpropagating through the unrolled predictions.
baselines: (Mentioned but not compared due to scaling) LSTM-based method (Srivastava et al., 2015)
metrics: Action classification accuracy using C3D on generated frames (proposed evaluation protocol)
Mean squared error (MSE) for affine-transform reconstruction / training objective (in transform/reconstruction space)
limitations: Underestimation of movement due to using MSE as the training criterion in transformation space.
Model assumptions are simplistic: ignores object identity effects, out-of-plane rotations and more general deformations.
Does not factor appearance (what) from motion (where); representation mixes object identity and motion.
Generating consistent sampled trajectories from a modeled distribution of transformations is challenging.
Authors report model produces sensible generations only up to about four frames.
_summary.topic_area: Computer Vision - video frame prediction / generative modeling
